summary: Martin defends Douglas Crockford after his disinvitation from Nodevember, framing it as an example of what he calls 'SJF' (Skulking Juvenile Fascist) behavior in the tech community. He argues that anonymous social media campaigns use fear and innuendo to pressure conference organizers into publicly shaming speakers over minor perceived offenses. Martin characterizes these tactics as cowardly, anti-social, and unjust, contrasting them with direct confrontation and debate. He proposes 'illumination' as the cure - forwarding critical tweets directly to their targets so they can respond. The post is a combative defense of free expression and a critique of what Martin sees as cancel culture in tech.
key_points: Conference organizers who capitulate to social media pressure become 'meat' for further campaigns, not allies
The tactics Martin criticizes involve indirect pressure on employers and associates rather than direct confrontation with the person
Martin sees these campaigns as fundamentally opposed to dialogue and understanding - 'They understand already!'
Transparency and direct communication are proposed as countermeasures to anonymous criticism
Martin frames himself as unbowed by similar threats, noting no 'dearth in invitations'
money_quotes: They won't confront you if you offend them. Oh no. They have no need to talk, debate, or try to come to an understanding. They understand already!
By that action, the organizers of Nodevember became SJF meat. The SJFs ate them for lunch.
Two words that they have no right to use, because their methods are both anti-social and unjust.
If you happen to come across the slime trail of an SJF, shine a light on it.
I've actually had a few SJFs make the same threat about me. So far I haven't noticed any dearth in invitations.
key_insight: Anonymous social media campaigns that pressure institutions rather than engaging individuals directly are fundamentally incompatible with honest discourse and justice.
